segments.s458-g33: که introduces a purpose/result clause ('so that' / 'that'). فرجام (noun) is the direct object meaning 'the end/outcome' and precedes the possessed phrase 'این رزم' (this battle) — literal order is 'the outcome of this battle'. بزم (noun) follows as the target state: the verb آوریم (1st pl. present/subjunctive) expresses a wish/optative meaning 'may we cause/bring about' (i.e. 'may we turn ... into a feast').
segments.s458-g34: مبادا is a negative optative/precautionary particle ('may it not be; lest'). که introduces the subordinate clause. دل (subject) is followed by the directional phrase سوی رزم and the verb آوریم in 1st plural again — here آوریم is used metaphorically ('cause/bring about') so the whole clause means 'lest (it be that) our hearts are brought toward battle' (i.e. 'lest our hearts turn to fighting').
segments.s458-g35: همه precedes the quality راستی (uprightness/truth) and is linked to the predicate باید ('must' / 'ought to be'); this is a poetic inversion where the noun phrase + باید expresses necessity ('all should be upright'). و connects the following noun مردمی, here meaning 'of the people' or 'for the people' as a coordinated element (elliptically: 'and (so) the people (should be so)').
segments.s458-g36: ز is the poetic preposition 'from / because of'. کژی و آزار form a coordinated object ('crookedness and harm'). خیزد is a present-tense verb meaning 'arise / begin to occur' and takes کمی (an adverb/quantifier 'a little') as its qualifier; word order places the source first ('from crookedness and harm') and ends with the extent ('a little').
segments.s458-g37: چو is a temporal/comparative conjunction 'when / as'. عموریه is the place-name 'Amorium'. کان here is an archaic/poetic temporal/locative particle ('then/thereupon' / 'then'), and نشست is the noun 'session/assembly' (or the act of sitting). منست is an adjectival/predicative form meaning 'was fitting/appropriate' — the clause reads as 'when the Amorium assembly sat, it was fitting.'
segments.s458-g38: تو آیی is a 2nd-person present/subjunctive or hortatory form ('you should/you come') used as an exhortation. و links the following noun سازی ('an instrument/implement' — here contextually a weapon). که introduces a relative clause whose verb گیری is 2nd-person present 'you take/seize'; بدست is a circumstantial phrase 'in (your) hand' — together 'and (you) come and take a weapon in hand.'
segments.s458-g39: دل من is the subject 'my heart'. به جوش آید is an idiomatic stative/verbal phrase ('is/comes to a boiling' = 'is stirred/filled with ardor'); آید is present/occurrent 'comes'. از introduces the cause 'from/because of' and links the coordinated nouns نام و ننگ ('name/reputation and naughtiness/shame') as the source of the passion.
segments.s458-g40: به هنگام بزم is a fronted temporal phrase 'at the time of the feast/banquet'. اندر is a locative/prepositional particle 'in/within' used before the verb. آیم is 1st-singular present/subjunctive or future-oriented 'I come / I will go' and here expresses the speaker's resolution to enter action. به جنگ is the directional complement 'into battle' (به + noun = 'to/into [battle]').
segments.s458-g73: بران بر نهادند is a compound past action: بر (on/upon) plus نهادند (they placed/set) gives the sense 'they imposed' or 'they set upon.' سالی is the direct object meaning 'a year' (i.e. a year's tribute/levy). که introduces the relative clause that follows ('that the shah ...'). The demonstrative/locative بران ('upon that' / 'thereon') anticipates the imposition.
segments.s458-g74: This is a relative clause modifying سالی. ستاند here is a past action of the shah toward the Caesar, but none of the offered lemma senses match perfectly, so the safer annotation is -1 for its specific sense; ز marks the source/agent 'from the Caesar', که begins a subordinate clause, دارد is a present/stative 'has/possesses', and سپاه is 'army'. Together: 'which the shah exacted/from the Caesar who has an army.'
segments.s458-g75: ز زر gives the material/source 'from/of gold.' خایه is the affected noun 'testicle(s)' and ریخته is a perfective/past participle 'poured/filled,' here functioning as the verb 'they poured/filled.' صدهزار is a quantitative numeral 'one hundred thousand' specifying the amount. Word order places the material first, then the patient, then the verb and quantity.
segments.s458-g76: ابا functions as a verb or verbal particle here (the exact lexical sense in the provided list does not fit the context, so it is marked -1); هر یکی = 'each one' (هر 'each' + یکی 'one'), گوهر 'gem' names the object, and شاهوار is an adjective 'kingly/regal.' Together this segment completes the action: 'and into each one a regal jewel.'
segments.s458-g77: چهل کرده مثقال is a numeric weight expression: چهل 'forty' + کرده (here part of a numeral/measure phrase, 'made/amounting to') + مثقال 'mithqal' (a unit of weight). هر خایه‌ای 'each testicle' is the noun phrase quantified by the preceding measure. The verb 'to weigh/amount to' is implicit in the poetic phrasing.
segments.s458-g78: همان 'that same' refers back to the previously mentioned jewel, نیز 'also/likewise' repeats or emphasizes, گوهر 'gem' names the item, and گرانمایه‌ای is an indefinite adjective 'a precious/valuable one.' The phrase means 'and likewise that same precious gem (each contained).'
segments.s458-g79: ببخشید is a past-tense verb here meaning 'they bestowed/gave' (the alternative sense 'to forgive' is not intended). بر marks the recipient 'upon/to,' مرزبانان is the plural 'frontier-guards/border governors,' and روم specifies 'of Rome/Byzantium.' The verb + بر + recipient sequence indicates the gifting action and its beneficiaries.
segments.s458-g80: هرانکس 'whoever/whosoever' begins a free relative clause; که 'that/who' introduces the clause, بودند is the past plural 'were' agreeing with مرزبانان (i.e. 'who among them were'), ز is the preposition 'from,' آباد 'prosperous/inhabited,' and بوم 'homeland/abode.' The whole clause narrows the recipients to 'whoever of them came from a prosperous homeland.'
segments.s458-g9: This phrase is a compact verb clause: the preposition ز (z) 'from' marks the origin (عموریه, Amorium), followed by the accusative/indefinite لشکری 'an army/troops' as the object, and the compound verbal idea گرد کرد 'gathered/assembled' (گرد + کرد). گرد here participates in the verbal construction meaning 'to gather', and کرد is the light verb 'to make/do' forming the past action.
segments.s458-g10: This is a noun phrase giving who was assembled: همه 'all' quantifies نامداران 'the famed/renowned ones', and روز نبرد 'the day of battle' is a temporal/genitive construction specifying purpose or occasion (literally 'day of battle'). Word order is typical Persian: quantifier + noun, then a temporal modifier.
segments.s458-g11: چو here introduces a temporal subordinate clause 'when'; دارا functions as the subject ('Dāra' / 'the possessor' used as a name/epithet) and بیامد is the past-tense intransitive verb 'came/arrived'. بزرگان روم is the following noun phrase 'the great men of Rome' (subject of the main clause).
segments.s458-g12: بپرداختند is a past-tense transitive verb in the 3rd-person plural 'they set about / they attended to / they made a move' and takes آن همه مرز و بوم as its object: آن همه 'that whole' modifies مرز و بوم 'frontier and land/country'. The construction places the verbal action before the full object phrase for poetic compactness.
segments.s458-g13: ز عموریه marks origin ('from Amorium'), followed by the coordinated subject فیلقوس و سران 'Filqus and the chiefs/leaders' — a simple noun coordination with و 'and'. In Persian epic diction names and titles are placed together before the verb.
segments.s458-g14: برفتند is the past 3rd-person plural 'they went/they set out', and گردان و جنگاوران is the coordinated noun phrase functioning as the accompaniment/companions 'the troop and the warriors'. Word order here places the verb before its accompanimental noun phrase in a concise, poetic style.
segments.s458-g15: This clause reports an event: دو 'two' quantifies رزم گران, a compound adjective/noun meaning 'fierce combats/warriors' (رزم 'battle' + گران 'heavy/fierce'), followed by کرده شد (the passive/perfect-like construction 'were made/occurred') and the temporal phrase در سه روز 'in three days'. The light verb کرده + شد produces the sense 'took place/occurred'.
segments.s458-g16: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'; بفروخت is a past tense verb here meaning 'he parted with / gave up' (used idiomatically to mean 'gave up the world' = died or relinquished life), and گیتی فروز is an epithet/name 'Giti‑froz' (literally 'world-illuminating'). The phrase reads 'on the fourth, when Giti‑froz gave up the world.'
segments.s458-g89: Number phrase + noun + postpositive quantifier + head noun with an attributive genitive: 'ده' (ten) modifies the following noun 'استر' (here an item name); 'همه' follows the noun and means 'all, every one' referring to those ten; 'بار' is the head noun 'load/cargo' and is specified by the genitive phrase 'دیبای روم' (brocade of Rome). Word order is typical of Persian poetic diction: numeral + item + modifier + head + genitive modifier.
segments.s458-g90: 'بسی پیکر' is a quantified nominal phrase where 'بسی' (much/many) modifies 'پیکر' (literally 'bodies' or 'lots/pieces' here meaning many consignments); 'از' introduces the material/source ('of/from'); 'گوهر و زر' is a coordinated noun phrase 'gems and gold'; 'بوم' names the place/source 'the homeland'. Together this gives 'many lots of gems and gold of the land.'
segments.s458-g91: 'شتروار سیصد' is a numeric/collective phrase: 'شتروار' (literally 'in camel-file' or 'in long file') characterizes the type of troupe/loads and 'سیصد' gives the number 'three hundred'. The following 'ز گستردنی' uses the poetic/genitive 'ز' ('of/from') with the participial adjective 'گستردنی' ('spread-out, extendable') describing the manner/arrangement (a caravan stretched out).
segments.s458-g92: This is a relative/content clause introduced by the poetic 'ز' ('of/consisting of') plus 'چیزی که' ('things that/which'): 'چیزی' is an indefinite 'something/things', 'که' introduces the relative clause, and 'بد راه را بردنی' describes those things as 'to be carried on the bad/rough road' — 'بد' = 'bad', 'راه' = 'road', 'را' is the direct-object/definite marker and 'بردنی' is the adjectival/participial form 'to be carried/portable'.
segments.s458-g93: 'دلارای رومی' is a nominal phrase: 'دلارای' ('beloved, charming') qualified by 'رومی' ('Roman') — a person described as the Roman beloved. 'به' is the preposition 'to/into' and 'مهد اندرون' is a compound locative ('maternity/cradle' used poetically for the inner chamber). Word order is noun phrase + prepositional phrase indicating movement into the inner apartments.
segments.s458-g94: Two agents are coordinated: 'سکوبا و راهب' ('Skuba' — an obscure term, likely a cupbearer — and 'a monk/rahib'). 'ورا' is a directional/adverbial pronoun 'thither/there (to her)', and 'رهنمون' is a noun 'guide' (used as the agential noun 'guides' or a verbal substantive). Together: 'the cupbearer and the monk guided her there.'
segments.s458-g95: Subject + adverbial/time + object + verb: 'کنیزک' is the subject 'a little maid/servant girl'; 'پس' is a temporal adverb 'then/afterward'; 'پشت ناهید' is the object 'the back of Nahid' (the possessor proper name follows the body-part); 'شست' is the past tense verb 'washed'. Persian often omits the object marker 'را' in poetic lines, so this reads 'the little maid then washed Nahid's back.'
segments.s458-g96: 'ازان' (from those/of those) introduces the source; 'هریکی' ('each one') is the distributive subject/agent; 'جامی از زر' is 'a cup/goblet of gold' (جامی = a cup, 'از زر' specifying material); 'بدست' is a prepositional/adverbial phrase 'in the hand' indicating that each held such a cup. The phrase thus means 'from those, each held a golden cup in hand.'
segments.s458-g41: تو آن کن is an imperative construction: تو (you) + آن (that, i.e. 'that thing') + کن (do!). The relative particle که introduces a relative clause meaning 'which/that', and از + شهریاران + سزاست literally means 'is fitting/of the kings' — سزاست is a present copular predicate 'it is fitting/deserved'. Overall: 'Do that which is fitting of kings.'
segments.s458-g42: This is a simple nominal clause with a past copula then a present predicate: پدر شاه بود uses بود in its past sense ('the father was king'), و is the coordinating 'and', and پسر پادشاست uses the present copula within the fused form پادشاست ('the son is a sovereign/monarch').
segments.s458-g43: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when'; بشنید is the past narrative 'he heard'. آزادگانرا is the object with the accusative marker -را ('the freemen'), and بخواند here is a past narrative meaning 'he called/summoned' or 'he caused to assemble' (خواند often = 'called out' in this context). The clause therefore reads 'When he heard, he summoned the freemen.'
segments.s458-g44: همه داستان is the direct object 'the whole tale/story'. پیش ایشان = 'before them / in their presence' (پیش = before/in the presence of). براند (from راند) is a narrative/formal verb here meaning 'he put/laid forth' or 'he related/presented' (classical usage), so the clause means 'he recited/presented the whole tale before them.'
segments.s458-g45: This segment reports content: چه is a relative/indefinite 'what/that which', بینید (here narrative past) 'they had seen', and گفت (used here as 'said/told') links the content to speech; اندرین گفت و گوی = 'in this saying/exchange and discourse' (اندرین = 'in these/among these', گفت و گوی = 'talk and discourse'). The whole means 'He related/told what they had seen in this conversation.'
segments.s458-g46: بجوید is a present/habitual verb 'he seeks/strives for' (here rendered as an ongoing action), همی is an emphatic adverb 'continually/still', فیلقوس is a personal name, and آب روی together function as the compound notion 'reputation/honor' (literally 'water-face' but idiomatically 'honour'). So: 'Filqus continually seeks honour.'
segments.s458-g47: همه مهتران is 'all the chiefs/leaders' (همه = all; مهتران = chiefs/great ones). خواندند is the past plural 'they cried/called out' and is followed by the interjection آفرین meaning 'bravo' or an acclamation. So: 'All the chiefs cried "Bravo!"'.
segments.s458-g48: که‌ای here functions as a vocative phrase ('که ای شاه' = 'O king' / 'you who are king'), شاه is the addressee 'king', بینادل is an adjective/epithet applied to the king (a rare or uncertain word in the manuscript but here used as praise), و is 'and', پاک‌دین is an epithet 'pure in faith' or 'devout'. The sequence addresses the king with two laudatory epithets.
segments.s458-g1: شد is a past/result verb here meaning 'happened/was brought' (poetic use: 'reached, spread'). از جنگ marks the source/starting point ('from the battle'); نیزه‌وران is the agent/group 'the spearmen' placed after از جنگ for emphasis. تا ... به روم gives the extent 'as far as/to Rome' (تا ... به = 'up to, as far as').
segments.s458-g2: همی is a continuous/adverbial particle ('still/kept'); جست (past of جستن/پریدن) here means 'leapt' or 'kept springing' and combines with همی to give repeated/continuous past ('kept leaping'). رزم is the object 'battle/combat'. اندر + آباد بوم is a locative phrase: اندر = 'into/in', آباد = 'inhabited/prosperous', بوم = 'land/homeland' — together 'into the inhabited land'.
segments.s458-g3: به روم اندرون is a prepositional/location phrase 'in the inner part/quarters of Rome' placed before the nominal phrase for poetic word order. شاه بدفیلقوس is the noun phrase 'King Badfilaqūs' (proper name) functioning as the subject/existential there was.
segments.s458-g4: کجا بود is an interrogative/exclamatory clause 'where was ...'; بود is the pastcopular verb 'was'. با رای او attaches as an ablative/instrumental phrase 'with his counsel/opinion' modifying the following noun. شاه سوس is the noun phrase 'King Sūs' (here a name), completing the question about that king's whereabouts or alignment.
segments.s458-g5: نوشتند is simple past, third-person plural 'they wrote'. نامه is the direct object 'a letter'. که introduces a reported/subordinate clause ('that...'). پور همای is a personal name phrase 'the son of Humāy' (پور = 'son/of the name-element', همای = name).
segments.s458-g6: سپاهی is the object 'an army/troops'. بیاورد is a subjunctive/jussive 3sg form of آوردن used in reported orders/requests ('should bring' / 'bring'). بی‌مر is an archaic/uncertain adverb here meaning roughly 'without delay' (contextually adverbial). ز جای is a prepositional phrase 'from (his) place'.
segments.s458-g7: چو is a subordinating conjunction 'when' placed before the verb. بشنید is past tense 'he heard'. سالار روم is the subject 'the commander (or chief) of Rome'. این سخن is the object 'this speech/word' that he heard.
segments.s458-g8: به یاد آمدش is a standard construction 'came to his memory' (به + noun 'to memory' + آمد + clitic -ش 'to him'). روزگار کهن is the noun phrase 'the ancient/olden time(s)' that returned to his memory.
segments.s458-g49: شهنشاه is the subject ('the sovereign, king of kings'). The phrase بر مهتران is a prepositional phrase with بر indicating superiority/‘over’ and مهتران (plural) 'the chiefs/elders' is the object of that preposition. مهتر here functions as a predicative adjective 'more eminent/greater' and است is the copula in the present, so the clause literally says 'The sovereign is more-mahter over the chiefs.'
segments.s458-g50: ز is the classical/poetic preposition 'from' (marking source or sphere), کار آن 'that affair/matter' is its object, and گزیند is the classical-present third-person singular of گزیدن 'he chooses'. The clause continues with an embedded locative clause introduced by the interrogative کجا 'where' and completed by در خور است ('is fitting/appropriate'); note that در خور is a compound meaning 'worthy/suitable' split for metrical reasons.
segments.s458-g51: The clause shows poetic inversion: یکی 'a certain' (indefinite) + دختری دارد 'has a daughter/maiden' with the possessor following the verb as این نامدار 'this famed one' (the subject). دارد is the present-tense verb 'has/possesses.'
segments.s458-g52: This is a descriptive phrase modifying the maiden: به بالای سرو uses به + بالای 'at the height/top of' plus سرو 'cypress' to produce the simile 'as tall as a cypress.' و links the second simile, به رخ چون بهار, where رخ 'face' + چون 'like' + بهار 'spring' yields 'with a face like spring.' The short preposition به here marks the comparison/location in both similes.
segments.s458-g53: بت‌آرای ('idol‑adorned' or 'idol-like beauty') + چون او ('such as she') frames the comparison. نبیند is negative + present form (ن- + بیند) meaning 'is not seen' or 'one does not see,' and به چین 'with a wrinkle' expresses the attribute absent on her—i.e. 'no wrinkle is seen on her.' The clause is impersonal/generic: people do not see a wrinkle on such a beauty.
segments.s458-g54: میان بتان 'among the beauties/ladies' (بتان used poetically for beautiful women) is the locative frame; چون introduces the simile, and درخشان نگین is a noun phrase 'a shining gem' functioning as the simile's vehicle: 'among beauties she is like a shining gem.' Word order places the simile after the frame for emphasis.
segments.s458-g55: اگر is the conditional 'if.' شاه is the subject; بیند is the present third‑person singular of دیدن 'he sees.' پسند is a noun 'liking/approval' and آیدش ('comes to him') is a compound where آید is 'comes' and -ش is the enclitic pronoun 'to him,' so پسند آیدش means 'approval comes to him' i.e. 'he finds her pleasing.'
segments.s458-g56: This is the result clause: به پالیز marks destination 'to the orchard,' and سرو بلند 'the tall cypress' is a poetic metaphor for the maiden; آیدش again is 'will come to him' (present/future sense in such contexts). The whole clause implies that if the king approves, the tall‑cypress (the maiden) will come to his garden/possession.
segments.s458-g81: ازان پس is a temporal phrase meaning 'from then on / thereafter' (ازان + پس). همه فیلسوفان شهر is a quantifier + noun phrase 'all the philosophers of the city'; in classical/poetic Persian the ezāfe linking 'philosophers' and 'city' is omitted, so شهر here functions as the genitive 'of the city.'
segments.s458-g82: هرانکس که introduces a free relative 'whoever' (هرانکس + relative particle که). بودش is the past tense 'was' with the enclitic pronoun -ش referring back to the person; ازان شهر gives the origin 'of/from that city' (again with no explicit ezāfe in poetry). بهر is a preposition 'for / toward / on behalf of' that signals purpose and connects this clause to what follows.
segments.s458-g83: بفرمود is an honorific/past reportive verb 'he commanded/said.' تا here functions as a subordinating particle meaning 'that / so that' introducing the result/purpose clause. راه را is the definite object ('the road') marked by را, and ساختند is the past plural 'they built.' The clause is a reported-order: 'he ordered that they built the road.'
segments.s458-g84: ز هر کار is a prepositional phrase 'to/for every task' (ز in poetic register = 'from/with/regarding'). دل را is the direct object 'the heart' (را marks definiteness/emphasis), and بپرداختند is a past plural verb literally 'they paid' but here idiomatically 'they devoted/attended (their hearts) to' — i.e., 'they applied themselves wholeheartedly.'
segments.s458-g85: برفتند is the past plural 'they went / departed.' با is the preposition 'with,' and دختر شهریار is the noun phrase 'the king's daughter' (poetic omission of the ezāfe linking دختر and شهریار is normal). The structure is verb + prepositional phrase indicating accompaniment.
segments.s458-g86: گرانمایگان هریکی is 'the eminent/illustrious ones, each one' (گرانمایگان = venerable persons; هریکی = each one). با نثار is 'with an offering' (با = with, نثار = gift/offering). Word order places the group and its distributive modifier before the instrumental accompaniment.
segments.s458-g87: یکی here is 'one' or 'one of them' (introducing an individual example). مهر زرین is a noun + adjective 'a golden seal' (مهر = seal/stamp; زرین = golden). بیاراستند is the past plural 'they adorned/ornamented' (بی-ārāst-ند). The clause reports a particular gift or act by one party.
segments.s458-g88: پرستنده تاجور is a compound noun phrase where پرستنده ('devout' or 'worshipping one') modifies تاجور ('merchant'); together 'a devout merchant.' خواستند is past plural 'they wanted / they asked for.' In context this likely means they requested or desired such a (devout) merchant — the exact role is determined by the broader narrative, but grammatically it's object phrase + past verb.
segments.s458-g25: به + place marks motion/toward or location; here 'به عموریه' introduces the place (to/at Amorium). در حصاری شدند is a verbless-location construction where در + noun (حصار) + شدن (شدند) indicates that they ended up 'in a fort/enclosure' — شدن (شدند) is the past plural of شدن used here to mean 'became/were placed' (they were/ended up). The word order places the place phrase before the locative inside-phrase for poetic compactness.
segments.s458-g26: از ایشان begins the second clause with 'from them' (agent/source). بسی is an adverb meaning 'much/many' in classical Persian (here 'many' or 'much'). زینهاری (a noun) means 'warnings/admonitions', and شدند (past plural of شدن) renders the clause passive/occurrence: literally 'many warnings came from them' or 'many warnings wereissued by them'. The sequence places source first (از ایشان) then the quantity/adverb and the noun, followed by the verb شدن.
segments.s458-g27: فرستاده‌ای آمد is 'a messenger came' — فرستاده (past-participle used as noun 'messenger/sent one') with the indefinite -ای, plus آمد (past) 'came'. از فیلقوس marks origin: 'from Philoqos'. Word order is subject (agent noun) + verb + origin phrase.
segments.s458-g28: This is a string of adjectival/descriptive phrases joined by و: خردمند و بیدار are coordinate adjectives 'wise and alert'. با introduces accompanying things: با نعم و بوس = 'with نعم and بوس' — here نعم means 'bounty/gifts' and بوس is the noun 'kiss(es)'. The adjectives and the با‑phrase further describe the messenger and what he carried.
segments.s458-g29: The clause lists items the messenger brought. برده here functions as a past participle 'having brought' or 'brought' attached to the preceding noun(s); the first token (ابا) appears as a nominal item (likely 'aba', a cloak/robe) though the lexical entry given does not record that sense, so the exact lexical identity is archaic/poetic. The coordinating و links the items, and با نثار introduces that the following things are given 'as offerings'.
segments.s458-g30: دو صندوق is a simple noun phrase 'two chests/boxes'. پرگوهر is a compound adjective 'full of gems / jewel‑studded' modifying صندوق, and شاهوار is a lofty/regal adjective further qualifying the chests. Word order is numeral + noun + (compound) adjective(s), typical in Persian poetry.
segments.s458-g31: چنین بود پیغام is 'such was the message' (چنین = 'thus/so', بود = past of 'to be', پیغام = 'message'). کز یک خدای is a relative/prepositional phrase 'that/from the one God' (کز = 'that/from which' here introducing the source or addressee). The clause sets up the content of the message.
segments.s458-g32: بخواهم is the present/subjunctive form 'I ask/wish' (classical subjunctive). که introduces a subordinate clause giving the content/desired outcome. او باشدم رهنمای uses the archaic optative/subjunctive باشدم ('may I be' / 'that he be') with او as the intended divine agent and رهنمای as predicate noun 'guide' — overall: 'I ask (of) the one God that he be my guide.'
segments.s458-g17: گریزان is a predicate adjective meaning 'fleeing' placed before the verb; بشد is the poetic past-form of شدن ('it became' / 'it happened') used here as the past copula, so the phrase means 'became fleeing' → 'fled'. فیلقوس و سپاه is the coordinated subject following the predicate, 'Filqus and the army.'
segments.s458-g18: یکی را is an emphatic/indefinite object phrase ('not even one' / 'no one'), with را marking the object; نبد is an archaic negative copula (contracted from نبُود) meaning 'was not' / 'there was not'; ترگ و رومی کلاه lists items lacking: 'a rag and a Roman hat/helm.' Word order places the emphatic 'one' before the negative copula for emphasis.
segments.s458-g19: زن و کودکان forms the coordinated patient ('women and children'); نیز ('also') modifies the whole clause; کردند is the past plural of کردن used causatively ('they made / caused'), so کردند اسیر = 'they took/ made (them) captives.' The object follows the verb phrase in this poetic order.
segments.s458-g20: بکشتند is the past plural verb 'they killed.' چندی is a quantifier here meaning 'several' or 'a number (of them)'; به functions instrumentally ('by/with') and links to شمشیر و تیر ('sword and arrow') as the weapons used.
segments.s458-g21: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'; از پیش here means 'from before / ahead' (indicating arrival in front), and دارا is the person (Dara) placed within the temporal phrase. به شهر آمدند is the past plural verb phrase 'came to the city' — plural آمدند can reflect Dara with retinue or a poetically plural verb.
segments.s458-g22: ازان 'from that / from there' heads the clause; رفته is a participle/adjective 'gone' describing the لشکر ('the army'), so 'the army that had gone'; دو لشکر 'two armies' is the subject and بهر آمدند means 'came for / toward (that purpose/place)' with آمدند the past plural 'they came.'
segments.s458-g23: دگر پیشتر places 'the others' and 'earlier' / 'before' as temporal/topic markers; کشته و خسته are two past-participial/adjectival predicates 'slain and weary' linked by و; بود is the past copula 'were' completing the predicate: 'the others had earlier been slain and were exhausted.'
segments.s458-g24: پس 'then' introduces the following situation; پشتشان is 'their back / rear' with the possessive suffix; نیزه پیوسته is a compound describing spears 'continuous / joined / set thickly'; بود is the past copula 'was' — together: 'then their rear was continuously/closely set with spears' (i.e. bristling with spears).
segments.s458-g57: This is a poetic word order: the definite object 'فرستاده روم را' (the Roman envoy) precedes the verb 'خواند' (he summoned/called), and the subject 'شاه' (the king) follows the verb. 'را' marks the definite object; 'خواند' is past tense 3rd‑person singular meaning 'called/summoned.'
segments.s458-g58: 'بگفت' is the narrative past 'he said.' The clause 'آنچ بشنید' ('that which he heard') supplies the content of what he said; 'آنچ' = 'that which' functions like a free relative, and 'بشنید' is past 'he heard.' 'از نیکخواه' indicates the source ('from the well-wisher').
segments.s458-g59: 'بدو گفت' = 'he said to him' (بد = to him + گفت = said). What follows is an instruction introduced by the imperative 'رو' (go), followed by the locative 'پیش قیصر' (in the presence of Caesar). 'بگوی' is a jussive/subjunctive imperative meaning 'tell/say' (go and tell him). The clause therefore issues the command: go and tell the Caesar.
segments.s458-g60: This is a conditional protasis introduced by 'اگر' ('if'). Here 'جست' is used with the sense 'seek' and 'خواهی' is the 2nd‑person present/future 'you want/will.' 'همی' is an emphatic/durative particle (poetic), and 'آب روی' literally 'water-face' functions idiomatically as 'the beauty/complexion' (i.e. beauty of her face). Together: 'if you seek the beauty (of her face)...'.
segments.s458-g61: 'پس' is a discourse adverb 'then/thereupon.' 'پرده تو' ('your curtain') places the possessor after the noun (poetic word order). 'یکی دختر است' = 'there is one / a certain maiden' (indefinite 'یکی' + noun), with the copula 'است' final.
segments.s458-g62: A relative clause introduced by 'که' ('who/that') modifies 'دختر.' 'بر تارک بانوان' = 'on the brow/crown of women' (prepositional phrase with 'بر'), and 'افسر است' is a metaphorical predicate: 'is a helmet/crest.' The whole clause says she is a crowning ornament among women.
segments.s458-g63: 'نگاری' here conveys 'a beauty/picture/likeness' (a poetic word for a lovely person). The relative 'که' introduces 'ناهید خوانی ورا' — 'you would call her Nahid' — where 'خوانی' is a poetic 2nd‑person verbal form meaning 'you call/name.' 'ورا' is the object pronoun 'her.'
segments.s458-g64: This continues the relative idea: 'بر اورنگ زرین' = 'on a golden throne' ('بر' = on, 'اورنگ' = throne, 'زرین' = golden). 'نشانی ورا' here functions as an archaic/poetic verbal idea 'you would set/seat her' with 'ورا' again the object 'her' (verb form is nominalized/poetic). The whole phrase: 'and you would place her upon a golden throne.'
segments.s458-g97: به + noun + اندرون here expresses location: به (toward/into) + جام (cup) + اندرون (inside) yields 'inside the cup.' گوهر (noun) follows and is qualified by the adjective شاهوار, so the phrase reads 'a shahvar (regal) gem inside the cup.' Word order is typical Persian: locative preposition before the noun phrase, adjective after the noun.
segments.s458-g98: بت‌آرای is an adjectival compound meaning 'adorned (as) an idol' (lit. 'idol‑adorning') modifying the gem. با introduces the accompaniment/with-phrase listing items of ornament. افسر here means a head‑ornament/diadem (classical usage 'helmet/crest' used as crown), و links the second item, گوشوار, an earring/ornamental pendant. The whole segment lists how the gem was set/displayed.
segments.s458-g99: سقف (literally 'roof, canopy') is used metonymically for a covering/ornament associated with the beautiful face (خوب رخ). را is the definite object marker marking 'سقف خوب رخ' as the direct object. به + دارا marks the recipient ('to the wealthy one/Dara'), and سپرد is the past tense 'entrusted/handed over.' The clause therefore means 'he entrusted the beautiful one's canopy to Dara/the wealthy one.'
segments.s458-g100: گهرها (the jewels) is the subject/object being acted on; به + گنجور او indicates direction/place 'into his treasury' (گنجور = treasure-house, او = his). برشمرد is past 'he counted/enumerated.' The verbless link between the two segments is narrative sequence: after handing over, he counted the gems into his treasury.
segments.s458-g101: ازان پس ('after that') sets the temporal frame. بران رزمگه ('of that battlefield' / 'thereon the battlefield') is a demonstrative/locative reference to the previously mentioned field; بس نماند is a negative past construction: بس (enough/no more) + نماند (did not remain) meaning 'no more remained' or 'there remained no more.' The overall sense is 'after that there remained nothing of that battlefield.'
segments.s458-g102: سپه را (the army, marked by object/contrast marker را) is the direct object of the verbal action expressed by براند. سوی شهر ایران ('toward the city of Iran') is the directional phrase. براند is the past tense of راندن 'to drive' (here 'he drove [the army]'). The clause thus states that he drove the host toward Iran's city.
segments.s458-g103: سوی پارس آمد is a simple past clause 'came toward Pars' (سوی = toward, پارس = Pars/Persia, آمد = came). دلارام is the subject (proper name) placed after the verb — a common poetic word order — and و شاد is a short coordination 'and (was) joyful,' where the copula 'was' is omitted (elliptical).
segments.s458-g104: کلاه بزرگی ('a large hat' — کلاه + بزرگی as a size/qualifier) is the object; بسر بر نهاد is an archaic/poetic rendering of بر سر نهاد 'put on (her) head' (بسر = بسَر = 'on the head', بر = on/upon, نهاد = placed/put, past). The verb نهاد is past tense 'placed/put,' so the whole segment means 'she put a great hat on her head.'
segments.s458-g65: This is a pair of coordinated imperatives. به من بخش is literally 'give/grant to me' with the preposition به before the pronoun من (prepositional object). و بفرست is the second imperative 'and send!' The phrase با باژ روم uses با ('with') + باژ ('a cry' or a shout) and then روم (Byzantium/Rum) as the destination — a poetic order where the instrument ('with a shout') is placed next to the destination, yielding 'send (her) to Rum with a shout.'
segments.s458-g66: چو خواهی که introduces a condition: چو + second‑person present خواهی 'if you wish' (چو = 'if/when' here). که introduces the content clause. بی‑رنج is a predicate adjective 'free from trouble/pain.' ماندت is the verb مانَد with the enclitic -ت (to you) — in classical word order the verb (with the dative clitic) can precede the noun it governs; بوم 'homeland/realm' is the thing hoped to remain. Together: 'if you wish that your bōm remain untroubled (for you).'
segments.s458-g67: فرستاده is the subject 'the messenger.' بَشنید is the simple past 'he heard' (third person). و آمد 'and came' links the two past actions. چو + باد is a simile 'like the wind' (چو = 'as/like', followed by the simile noun باد). The sequence describes quick action: he heard and then rushed off 'like the wind.'
segments.s458-g68: به قیصر is a dative phrase 'to Caesar.' بر آن is 'on/over/about that' (بر + demonstrative آن) introducing the topic. گفتها is a noun/archaic form 'utterances' or 'what was said'; کرد is a light verb 'made/did' combining with گفتها to mean 'uttered/said' or 'recounted.' یاد here functions as 'remembrance/mention' — the compound thus means 'he mentioned/recounted that to Caesar.' Note the noun + کردن (گفتها کرد) pattern common in classical Persian.
segments.s458-g69: بدان is a prepositional phrase 'at that' (ب + آن contracted in classical usage). شاد شد is the past 'became glad/rejoiced.' The subjects فیلقوس و سپاه follow the predicate — a common poetic inversion where the comment ('at that') and predicate come before the subject: 'At that, Filqūs and the army rejoiced.'
segments.s458-g70: که introduces the explanatory clause 'for/since.' داماد باشد is a subordinate clause with باشد (subjunctive/optative sense here) meaning 'would be / be made a son‑in‑law.' مر is a dative pronoun ('to me' / 'for me') appearing before the object او را in a poetic order; او را is the marked object 'him.' چو شاه is a simile 'as/like a king' modifying the benefit conferred. Altogether: 'because he would be made son‑in‑law — to me/him as a king' (poetic word order and enclitic dative produce this slightly compressed phrasing).
segments.s458-g71: سخن گفت is the light verbal phrase 'spoke/uttered words' (سخن + گفت[ن]). هرگونه 'in every way / of every kind' modifies the speech. از here means 'about/concerning' and introduces the topics باژ و ساو (names or terms here). The order noun+verb+modifier+از + topics is typical of classical/poetic Persian.
segments.s458-g72: ز (poetic form of از) begins a prepositional phrase 'about/from.' چیزی 'anything/something' is the head of the relative clause introduced by که 'that.' دارد is the present 'has' used idiomatically with پی 'after/in pursuit of' to mean 'that pertains to the pursuit of.' روم is the object 'Rome/Rum,' and تاو here follows as the referent (likely a personal name, Tâv) — so the relative clause reads 'that pertains to Tâv's pursuit of Rum.' Note the compressed, suffixless classical constructions where possession/association is shown by placing the possessor after the thing possessed in apposition.

sentences.s458-s17: that we may turn the outcome of this battle into a feast; lest our hearts be drawn toward battle.
sentences.s458-s18: There must be complete uprightness, and may little crookedness and harm arise among the people.
sentences.s458-s19: When the Amorium assembly sat, it was fitting; you should come and take a weapon in hand.
sentences.s458-s20: My heart boils from (thoughts of) fame and shame; at the time of the feast I will enter into battle.
sentences.s458-s37: They imposed upon them a year's tribute, which the shah took from the Caesar who had an army.
sentences.s458-s38: From gold they poured (in) one hundred thousand into the testicles,
sentences.s458-s39: each testicle weighed (or held) forty mithqals, and likewise each [contained] that same precious jewel.
sentences.s458-s40: They bestowed them upon the Roman frontier-guards — whoever among them was from a prosperous homeland.
sentences.s458-s5: He raised an army from Amorium, gathering all the famed warriors for the day of battle.
sentences.s458-s6: When Dāra arrived, the great men of Rome set about that whole frontier and country.
sentences.s458-s7: From Amorium Filqus and the chiefs set out with their troop and warriors.
sentences.s458-s8: Two fierce combats occurred in three days; on the fourth, when Giti‑froz gave up the world.
sentences.s458-s45: Ten loads, all laden with Roman brocade; many consignments of gems and gold from the homeland.
sentences.s458-s46: Three hundred in long array (camel-loads), spread out — of things that were to be carried over the rough road.
sentences.s458-s47: They brought the Roman beloved into the inner cradle/chamber; the cupbearer and the monk guided her there.
sentences.s458-s48: A little maid then washed Nahid's back; from those (items) each held a golden cup in hand.
sentences.s458-s21: Do what befits kings: the father was a king and the son is a sovereign.
sentences.s458-s22: When he heard, he summoned the freemen and recited the whole tale before them.
sentences.s458-s23: He told (them) what they had seen in this exchange of words, and Filqus continually sought reputation (honor).
sentences.s458-s24: All the chiefs cried 'Bravo!' — 'O king, (you are) noble-hearted and pure in faith.'
sentences.s458-s1: The spearmen's fighting spread from the battlefield as far as Rome; battle kept leaping into the inhabited land.
sentences.s458-s2: In Rome's inner quarters was King Badfilaqūs; where was King Sūs with his counsel?
sentences.s458-s3: They wrote a letter that the son of Humāy should bring soldiers from his place without delay.
sentences.s458-s4: When the commander of Rome heard this speech, the ancient times came to his memory.
sentences.s458-s25: The sovereign is superior to the chiefs; from that affair he chooses where it is fitting.
sentences.s458-s26: This famed man has a maiden, tall as a cypress and with a face like spring.
sentences.s458-s27: In idol‑beauty such as hers no wrinkle is seen; among beauties she is like a shining gem.
sentences.s458-s28: If the king sees (her) and takes a liking, she will come to his orchard as a tall cypress.
sentences.s458-s41: After that, all the city's philosophers — whoever among them was from that town —
sentences.s458-s42: He commanded that they build the road, and they set their hearts to every task.
sentences.s458-s43: They departed with the king's daughter; the illustrious ones, each with an offering.
sentences.s458-s44: One (of them) adorned a golden seal; they sought a devout merchant.
sentences.s458-s13: They took refuge in the fortress at Amorium; from them many warnings were issued.
sentences.s458-s14: A messenger came from Philoqos, wise and alert, with gifts and kisses.
sentences.s458-s15: He had brought an aba and a badreh, and, as offerings, two jewel-filled, regal chests.
sentences.s458-s16: The message was thus: 'I ask the one God that he be my guide.'
sentences.s458-s9: Filqus and the army fled; not a single man had even a rag or a Roman helmet.
sentences.s458-s10: They also made women and children captives, and they killed several with sword and arrow.
sentences.s458-s11: When Dara came into the city, two armies that had gone from there came toward it.
sentences.s458-s12: Others had been killed and were exhausted earlier; then their rear was bristling with spears.
sentences.s458-s29: The king summoned the Roman envoy and said what he had heard from the well-wisher.
sentences.s458-s30: He told him, 'Go before the Caesar and say: if you seek the beauty of her face...'
sentences.s458-s31: Behind your curtain there is a maiden who is like a helmet upon the brow of women.
sentences.s458-s32: A likeness that you would call Nahid, and whom you would set upon a golden throne.
sentences.s458-s49: Inside the cup lay a regal jewel, idol‑adorned, with a diadem and ear‑pendants.
sentences.s458-s50: He handed the fair one's canopy to the wealthy one and counted the jewels into his treasury.
sentences.s458-s51: After that, no remnant of that battlefield remained; he drove the army toward the city of Iran.
sentences.s458-s52: Dolaram came toward Pars, joyful, and placed a large hat upon her head.
sentences.s458-s33: Grant (her) to me and send (her) to Rum with a shout, if you wish that your realm remain free of trouble.
sentences.s458-s34: The messenger heard and came like the wind; to Caesar he recounted that (matter).
sentences.s458-s35: At that Filqūs and the army rejoiced, for he would be a son‑in‑law — to him like a king.
sentences.s458-s36: He spoke in every manner of Bâzh and Sâv, about anything that concerned Tâv's pursuit of Rum.
